narrative: As an Asian male applying to law school, you've put in significant effort, boasting a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.40—which is actually above the typical 3.21 average—and a competitive LSAT score of 36, just shy of the usual 36.70 benchmark. You're planning to attend full-time, like the vast majority of students at 92.7%, and come from a lower-middle family income bracket. However, your Year 1 school decile of 2 and especially your Year 3 decile of 1 are notably lower than the averages of around 5.7 and 5.5, reflecting grades that placed you in the bottom tiers compared to peers. The admissions model, trained on past students' data to predict first-time bar exam success, estimated your failure risk at 51.8%, tipping just over the threshold that led to your application's rejection. This prediction hinges on a few key factors from your profile, with your Year 3 academic decile standing out as the most influential, strongly pushing the model toward expecting a bar failure because it signals consistently lower performance relative to others. Your LSAT score played the next biggest role, but in a positive way, nudging the prediction toward success by showing strong test-taking ability aligned with those who pass. Similarly, your undergraduate GPA provided a helpful counterbalance, pulling toward a pass prediction thanks to its above-average strength. Overall, while your LSAT and GPA helped mitigate concerns, they weren't enough to offset the dominant impact of your lower Year 3 decile, which the model sees as the strongest indicator of bar struggles in cases like yours. Compared to typical students who get approved, your profile mirrors many but stands out with those early-to-mid undergrad deciles dragging down the outlook more than your other solid metrics. To move forward, consider retaking courses or seeking targeted tutoring to boost your decile rankings, perhaps through a post-bac program, and reapply after demonstrating improved academic momentum—many students in similar situations turn things around this way. Admissions teams value persistence, especially from determined applicants like you, so use this as fuel to strengthen your record.
